title: High-Dimensional Theory of LoRA Fine-Tuning in a Solvable Attention Model
authors: O. Duranthon, F. Boncoraglio, L. Zdeborová
abstract: We develop a high-dimensional statistical theory of low-rank adaptation (LoRA) in attention models, capturing the interplay between pre-training and fine-tuning. We introduce a solvable framework in which a single-head attention layer is first pre-trained on a data-abundant task and subsequently adapted via a rank-one LoRA update on limited data. In the high-dimensional limit, both stages admit a sharp asymptotic characterization in terms of a finite set of order parameters, yielding explicit predictions for test errors and representation alignment. Our analysis shows that the impact of pre-training on LoRA is summarized by an effective noise term, from which we derive prescriptions for the optimal pre-training procedure. We also demonstrate a regime with a mismatch between the value of the test error and representation quality, and propose an application of our theory to active fine-tuning.
summary: This paper establishes a comprehensive high-dimensional statistical theory for understanding how low-rank adaptation (LoRA) fine-tuning performs after extensive pre-training. By mapping the problem onto order parameters derived from free energy minimization, it quantifies performance losses via an 'effective noise' term. The results offer actionable insights into optimizing both the pre-training and subsequent fine-tuning regimes.
main_problem: The paper develops a high-dimensional statistical theory to characterize Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) fine-tuning in attention models, analyzing the interplay between extensive pre-training and limited fine-tuning data.
main_result: The performance of LoRA is characterized by an effective noise term derived from order parameters, providing explicit predictions for test errors and suggesting optimal procedures like active fine-tuning or map recalibration.
method: The analysis employs high-dimensional statistical physics techniques, including the replica method and free entropy maximization, to derive asymptotic characterizations of learning dynamics in the limit of infinite dimensions.
model_or_system: The model is a single-head attention layer undergoing pre-training on abundant data followed by fine-tuning via a rank-one LoRA update. The analysis focuses on Gaussian input sequences for tractability.
key_observables: Test errors ($E', E$), representation overlap, effective noise ($\Delta_{	ext{eff}}$), and order parameters (e.g., $Q, M$).
important_parameters: High-dimensional limits ($D 	o \infty$), pre-training regularization strength ($\lambda$), fine-tuning sample size ratio ($ho$), and rescaling factors ($ eta$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the high-dimensional limit, Gaussian input sequences for tractability, and specific structural assumptions about the attention mechanism (e.g., tied keys/queries).
figures_summary: Figures illustrate how recalibrating the frozen pre-trained map ($\hat{W}$) using a factor $ eta/ eta^\star$ improves downstream LoRA transfer, and show the effect of pre-training on fine-tuning performance parameterized by effective noise.
paper_structure: The paper builds from foundational high-dimensional theory (defining order parameters) to deriving state-evolution equations for both training stages. It then applies this framework to analyze specific phenomena like active fine-tuning, map recalibration, and the comparison against Bayes-optimal benchmarks.
why_interesting: This work bridges statistical physics tools (replica theory, free energy minimization) directly into modern deep learning optimization problems (LoRA), providing rigorous theoretical guarantees on generalization bounds in high dimensions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Fine-tuning test error E′ versus pre-training regularization λ for λ′ ∈{0.05, 1, ∞}, at α = 0.1, α′ = 3, T = 3, κ0 = κ = 1, and noise levels ∆= 0.5 (left) and ∆= 2 (right). Insets show E′ versus λ′ at the optimal λ. Solid lines are state-evolution predictions; square markers are D = 150 simulations averaged over four runs. Vertical dark-red dashed lines mark curve minima, and the black dashed line is the Bayes-optimal benchmark. The cases λ′ = ∞and λ = ∞correspond respectively to no LoRA adaptation ( ˆw = 0) and LoRA-only fine-tuning ( ˆW = 0).

Figure 2: Effect of pre-training on the fine-tuning task. Fine-tuning test error E′ vs ∆eff = ∆/2 + Q0 −2M + Q, parameterized by fixing M = 1 and varying Q0 = Q. Left: ∆= 0; right: ∆= 2. Lines are the theoretical predictions; dots are simulations at D = 104 over five independent runs. T = 3, λ′ = 0.5, σ softmax.

Figure 3: Fine-tuning input samples form the pre-training set e = 1. Left: pre-training train error L and test error E; center: fine-tuning test error E′; right: fine-tuning overlap ow; vs regularizations λ and λ′. Lines are the theoretical predictions; dots are simulations at D = 103

Figure 4: Active fine-tuning. Left: fine-tuning test errors E′( ˆW, ˆw) and E′( ˆW, ˆwActFT); inset: difference between the test errors ∆E′; right: overlaps ow; vs ∆eff = ∆/2 + Q0 −2M + Q, parameterized by fixing M = 1 and varying Q0 = Q; for different labeling ratios ρ. Lines are the theoretical predictions; dots are simulations at D = 104 over five independent runs. We minimize E′ over λ′ ∈{0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1} for each point. ¯α′ = 100, T = 3, ∆= 0, σ softmax.

Figure 5: Effect of pre-training on the fine-tuning task for the rescaled identity activation σ : x 7→x/T. Fine-tuning test error E′ versus ∆eff = ∆/2 + Q0 −2M + Q. Left: test error; right: excess test error with respect to the value at ∆eff = 0. Lines are theoretical predictions; dots are simulations at D = 5 × 103 over five independent runs. T = 3.
